Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Baker, Samuel Cranage

Facts.Text: Samuel Cranage Baker was appointed as Chief Executive Officer of StreetSine.
Shareholders’ Agreement between SPHI and the plaintiffs was executed.
Management agreements for Mr Baker and Mr Lee between them and SSTG were executed.
Engagement of Mr Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO of StreetSine was formalized.
Termination of the plaintiffs’ employments as Executive Director and CTO was approved by the SSTG board.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the plaintiffs were unfairly excluded from management roles in contravention of their legitimate expectations.
Party_Details.Rule: A minority shareholder is entitled to fair treatment, an ambulatory standard bounded by the four limbs of s 216(1) of the Companies Act.
Party_Details.Application: The plaintiffs argued that they had a legitimate expectation to remain in management roles, while the defendants contended that the termination was in the best interests of the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the plaintiffs had no legitimate expectation to remain in management roles after 30 June 2018, and the decision to terminate them was taken in good faith with the best interests of StreetSine in mind.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: SPH Interactive Pte. Ltd.

Facts.Text: SPH Interactive Pte. Ltd. acquired 60% of the shares in SSTG.
Shareholders’ Agreement between SPHI and the plaintiffs was executed.
Engagement of Mr Jason Lewis Barakat-Brown as CEO of StreetSine was formalized.
Termination of the plaintiffs’ employments as Executive Director and CTO was approved by the SSTG board.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether SPHI acted oppressively by excluding the plaintiffs from management roles.
Party_Details.Rule: A majority shareholder is entitled to make management decisions in good faith in the best interests of the company.
Party_Details.Application: SPHI argued that the decision to terminate the plaintiffs was made in good faith and was necessary for the company's best interests.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that SPHI acted within its rights as a majority shareholder and the decision to terminate the plaintiffs was not oppressive.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: SPH Interactive Pte. Ltd.

Facts.Text: SPH Interactive Pte. Ltd. and the founders executed a Share Purchase Agreement (SPA).
Under Clause 8.2 of the SPA, the founders agreed to pay to SSTG or SSSPL the sum of any tax liability arising before 31 October 2014, and payments connected to that tax liability.
IRAS assessed an additional tax liability for the year 2013 against SSTG, resulting in a net payment by the company of $4,406.93.
Regnum Corporate Services Pte Ltd invoiced SSTG for the sum of $6,420.00 for advice and correspondence with IRAS in connection with the 2013 tax liability.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the vendors of a company are legally responsible for pre-acquisition tax liabilities and associated corporate advisory costs pursuant to a tax indemnity clause in a share purchase agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: A contracting party is bound by express indemnity clauses to cover specific historical liabilities, such as pre-completion tax assessments, arising out of a corporate acquisition.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff by counterclaim argued that the Defendants by counterclaim were bound by Clause 8.2 of the SPA to cover the 2013 tax liabilities and associated advisory costs. The Defendants by counterclaim put the Plaintiff by counterclaim to strict proof of the amounts but did not raise a positive substantive defence.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court noted that during cross-examination, Mr Lee accepted the amounts claimed. The court found that SPHI had successfully proved the net additional tax paid to IRAS and the advisory costs incurred. Accordingly, the court allowed the counterclaim and ordered the founders to pay the respective sums.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Baker, Samuel Cranage

Facts.Text: Samuel Cranage Baker and Jeremy Lee Chuen Yang executed a Share Purchase Agreement (SPA) with SPH Interactive Pte. Ltd.
Clause 8.2 of the SPA contained a provision requiring the founders to cover tax liabilities arising prior to the completion date.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a vendor is legally responsible to reimburse a purchaser for historical tax assessments and related advisory fees under a share purchase agreement indemnity.
Party_Details.Rule: A contracting party is bound by express indemnity clauses to cover specific historical liabilities, such as pre-completion tax assessments, arising out of a corporate acquisition.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendants by counterclaim did not raise a positive defence in their pleadings or submissions, but merely put the opposing party to strict proof of the counterclaim. The Plaintiff by counterclaim stated the SPA strictly required indemnification for these specific 2013 costs.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the founders were liable under Clause 8.2 of the SPA. Because the founders admitted the amounts during cross-examination and the documentary evidence proved the tax and advisory costs, the court ordered them to pay the sums.
